# Fix Remaining Audit Issues (R4) — Push to Full Pass You are working on a Spring Boot + Thymeleaf retail campaign governance application that has gone through multiple fix rounds and is now at **Partial Pass** with no Blockers remaining. **Everything runs through Docker — no local JDK or Maven.** This prompt addresses the 5 High, 2 Medium, and 1 Low findings from the attached audit report to reach a full Pass. ## How to Build, Run, and Test ```bash # Run the app docker compose up --build # Run tests docker compose -f docker-compose.test.yml run --build test # Stop docker compose down ``` App at http://localhost:8080. No manual `.env` setup needed. ## Critical Rules 1. **Docker only.** Never use `mvn`, `./mvnw`, `java`, `javac`, or any local commands. 2. **Never edit existing Flyway migrations.** Use the next available version number for new ones. 3. **Preserve all existing working flows.** Prior rounds fixed: pre-auth lockout/CAPTCHA, dual-approval queue, coupon CRUD, campaign file object auth, sensitive access logging, campaign analytics, masked downloads, URL content import, finance role scoping, AES encryption service, audit log immutability (DB triggers), backup password safety, rollback confirmation UX. Do NOT break any of these. 4. **Work in priority order:** High → Medium → Low. 5. **After ALL fixes**, verify: ```bash docker compose down docker compose up --build ``` Then: `docker compose -f docker-compose.test.yml run --build test` — all tests must pass. --- ## HIGH #1 — Anti-replay/signing does not protect the actual approval completion endpoint used by the UI **Problem:** The `NonceValidationFilter` scope covers `/admin/**` and `/approval/dual-approve/**`, but the UI template (`approval/queue.html:86`) actually posts to `POST /approval/{id}/approve-second` (`ApprovalController.java:77`), which is OUTSIDE the nonce filter scope. The nonce-protected endpoint at `ApprovalController.java:126` (`/approval/dual-approve/{requestId}`) exists but is NOT the one the UI uses. So the real high-risk dual-approval completion path has zero anti-replay protection. **Evidence:** `NonceValidationFilter.java:93` (scope: `/admin/**` + `/approval/dual-approve/**`), `ApprovalController.java:77` (`/{id}/approve-second` — unprotected), `templates/approval/queue.html:86` (UI posts to unprotected path), `ApprovalController.java:126` (protected but unused by UI). **Fix:** - **Option A (recommended — simplest):** Add `/approval/*/approve-second` and `/approval/*/approve-first` to the `NonceValidationFilter` and `RequestSigningFilter` URL scope. Then update the approval queue template JS to fetch a nonce and sign these requests before submitting (same pattern as any other signed endpoint in the app). - **Option B:** Change the UI template to POST to the already-protected `/approval/dual-approve/{requestId}` endpoint instead, and remove or deprecate the unprotected `approve-second` endpoint. - Whichever option you choose, **verify that both first-approval and second-approval endpoints are covered** by anti-replay protection. - Add integration tests (see test section) proving replay is rejected on these endpoints. --- ## HIGH #2 — Admin browser form mutations still bypass request-signing/nonce **Problem:** `NonceValidationFilter` and `RequestSigningFilter` both have `shouldNotFilter` bypasses for `application/x-www-form-urlencoded` and `multipart/form-data` content types under `/admin/**`. This means all admin form POSTs (user create/update/deactivate, role changes, backup, restore) skip anti-replay/signing. **Evidence:** `NonceValidationFilter.java:105,110`, `RequestSigningFilter.java:95,100`. **Fix — narrow the bypass to only truly low-risk endpoints:** 1. Instead of bypassing ALL form-encoded `/admin/**` POSTs, create an explicit allowlist of low-risk admin form endpoints that may skip signing (e.g., simple search/filter forms that don't mutate state). 2. High-risk admin mutations MUST go through signing. These include at minimum: - `POST /admin/users` (create/update user) - `POST /admin/users/{id}/deactivate` - `POST /admin/users/{id}/role` (role changes) - `POST /admin/backup/{id}/restore` 3. For these high-risk form endpoints, implement browser-side signing: - Add a shared `admin-signing.js` (if not already present) that intercepts form submissions on high-risk admin forms. - Before submit: fetch a nonce from `GET /admin/nonce`, compute signature, convert the form to a `fetch()` request with `Content-Type: application/json` (converting form data to JSON body), and include `X-Nonce`, `X-Timestamp`, `X-Signature` headers. - This way the request is no longer `application/x-www-form-urlencoded` and won't hit the bypass. 4. Update the corresponding controller methods to accept JSON `@RequestBody` in addition to or instead of form params for these high-risk endpoints. 5. Update `SecurityDesignDecisions.md` to document which admin endpoints require signing and which are exempt (with justification). 6. Keep the form-encoded bypass ONLY for genuinely low-risk/read-only admin operations. --- ## HIGH #3 — Approval workflow does not enforce stacking/mutual-exclusion validation **Problem:** `CouponService.checkStackingCompatibility()` exists (line 110) but is never called during the approval flow. A reviewer can approve a campaign without the system verifying that its coupons don't conflict with already-active campaigns' stacking/exclusion rules. **Evidence:** `CouponService.java:110` (method defined, only static occurrence — never invoked by approval flow), `ApprovalService.java` (no call to stacking check in approve methods). **Fix:** 1. In `ApprovalService`, in the method that processes approval decisions (the approve/complete path), add a **pre-approval validation hook**: - Before changing status to APPROVED, call `couponService.checkStackingCompatibility(campaign)` (or whatever the method signature requires — inspect it first). - If the check fails (stacking conflict detected), **reject the approval** with a clear error message explaining which campaigns/coupons conflict. - Return this error to the controller so the UI can display it to the reviewer. 2. Update the approval UI (`approval/queue.html` or a detail page) to display stacking validation errors when an approval is rejected by the system. 3. Add a unit test: campaign with conflicting stacking rules → approval attempt → fails with stacking error. 4. Add a unit test: campaign with no conflicts → approval succeeds. --- ## HIGH #4 — Analytics issuance metric is semantically incorrect and ignores filters **Problem:** `AnalyticsService.java:37` computes "issuance" as the sum of `maxUses` across ALL coupons via `couponRepository.findAll()`, completely ignoring date range and store filters. This produces misleading KPI values. **Evidence:** `AnalyticsService.java:37`. **Fix:** 1. Change the issuance calculation to respect the same filters (date range, store) applied to other analytics metrics: - Add a repository query method like `couponRepository.sumMaxUsesByCampaignFilters(storeId, fromDate, toDate)` that joins coupons to their campaigns and filters by store and date range. - Replace the current `findAll()` sum with this filtered query. 2. If "issuance" should represent actual coupon distribution events rather than `maxUses` capacity: - Define what constitutes an "issuance event" (e.g., coupon assigned to customer, coupon code generated). - If such events don't exist yet, use `maxUses` as a documented proxy but make it filter-aware. - Add a comment in the service explaining the metric definition. 3. Add a unit/integration test with deterministic fixtures: - Create campaigns with coupons in different stores and date ranges. - Call analytics with specific filters. - Assert the issuance value only includes matching coupons, not all. --- ## HIGH #5 — Weekly restore-test automation is not implemented **Problem:** `RestoreService.java:100` has a `testRestoreLatest()` method but it is never scheduled. The prompt requires weekly automated restore testing. Only nightly backup has `@Scheduled`. **Evidence:** `BackupService.java:64` (nightly backup scheduled), `RestoreService.java:100` (restore drill method exists but unscheduled), grep for `@Scheduled` in restore service = no matches. **Fix:** 1. Add a `@Scheduled` annotation to a new method in `RestoreService` (or a dedicated `RestoreDrillScheduler` component) that runs weekly: ```java @Scheduled(cron = "0 0 3 ? * SUN") // Every Sunday at 3 AM public void weeklyRestoreDrill() { try { RestoreResult result = testRestoreLatest(); auditLogService.log("RESTORE_DRILL", "SYSTEM", Map.of("success", result.isSuccess(), "duration", result.getDuration())); if (!result.isSuccess()) { // Log warning / trigger alert log.error("Weekly restore drill FAILED: {}", result.getErrorMessage()); } } catch (Exception e) { log.error("Weekly restore drill exception", e); auditLogService.log("RESTORE_DRILL_FAILED", "SYSTEM", Map.of("error", e.getMessage())); } } ``` 2. Ensure `testRestoreLatest()` updates `BackupRecord.restoredAt` on success (it may already do this — verify). 3. Ensure the `@Scheduled` annotation is picked up by adding `@EnableScheduling` on a config class if not already present (check `SecurityConfig` or a dedicated `SchedulingConfig`). 4. Add a test that verifies the `weeklyRestoreDrill` method invokes `testRestoreLatest` and logs the result (mock the restore execution, assert audit log entry). --- ## MEDIUM #6 — "Why blocked" permission explanations inconsistent across screens **Problem:** Some pages show disabled buttons with tooltips explaining why an action is blocked (`campaign/list.html:22-31,111-116`), and a generic 403 page exists. But many restricted actions are simply hidden via `sec:authorize` in the sidebar and other templates, with no contextual explanation for why the user doesn't see them. **Evidence:** `templates/campaign/list.html:22-31,111-116` (good — disabled + tooltip), `templates/error/403.html:16-21` (generic 403), `templates/layout/sidebar.html` (hidden via `sec:authorize` — no explanation). **Fix:** 1. For the **sidebar**: this is acceptable UX — hiding nav items for inaccessible sections is standard. No change needed here. 2. For **action buttons within pages** the user CAN access (campaign list, approval queue, coupon list, file management, analytics): - Instead of hiding action buttons entirely with `sec:authorize`, show them in a **disabled state with a tooltip** explaining the permission requirement. - Pattern: `` - Apply this to at minimum: campaign edit/delete buttons, approval action buttons, coupon edit buttons, export buttons, admin action buttons visible on shared pages. 3. For the **403 error page**: enhance with a more specific message if possible. Include the user's current role and suggest which role is needed: ```html

Your current role does not have permission to perform this action.

If you believe you should have access, contact your administrator.

``` 4. Keep the existing disabled+tooltip patterns as the standard and extend them to pages that currently just hide buttons. --- ## MEDIUM #7 — Encryption-at-rest applied too narrowly (only backup file path) **Problem:** The `@Convert(converter = EncryptedStringConverter.class)` annotation is only applied to `BackupRecord.filePath`. No other sensitive fields use it. The prompt requires encryption at rest for critical secrets. **Evidence:** `BackupRecord.java:31` (only field with converter), grep for `EncryptedStringConverter` = only BackupRecord. **Fix:** 1. Identify additional sensitive fields that should be encrypted at rest. At minimum: - `User` entity: any stored tokens, security questions, or PII beyond username (check the entity). - `CouponCode` or similar: if coupon codes are considered sensitive/redeemable. - `TempDownloadLink` entity: the token/URL field. - Any API keys or external credentials stored in DB. 2. Apply `@Convert(converter = EncryptedStringConverter.class)` to each identified field. 3. Add a Flyway migration if column types need widening for Base64 ciphertext (e.g., `ALTER TABLE ... MODIFY COLUMN ... TEXT`). 4. Add or update `SecurityDesignDecisions.md` with a **data classification section** listing which fields are encrypted and why, and which are NOT encrypted with justification (e.g., "username is not encrypted because it is used for login lookups and is not considered a secret"). 5. Test that existing data flows still work after adding encryption (create/read cycles for affected entities). --- ## LOW #8 — README is Docker-centric with no local fallback steps **Problem:** README only shows Docker commands for run/test. A static reviewer without Docker has no quick path. **Evidence:** `README.md:5`, `README.md:12`. **Fix:** - Add an optional section to `README.md`: ```markdown ## Local Development (Optional — without Docker) Prerequisites: JDK 17+, Maven 3.8+, MySQL 8.0+ ```bash # Set environment variables (or create .env and source it) export DB_HOST=localhost DB_PORT=3306 DB_NAME=retail_campaign export DB_USERNAME=retail_user DB_PASSWORD=retail_pass # Run tests ./mvnw test # Start the app ./mvnw spring-boot:run ``` Note: Docker is the recommended and fully supported path. Local setup may require additional MySQL configuration. ``` - Keep this brief. The Docker path remains primary. --- ## Required New/Updated Tests All run via `docker compose -f docker-compose.test.yml run --build test`: 1. **Anti-replay on approval endpoints:** Integration test — `POST /approval/{id}/approve-second` (or whichever endpoint the UI uses) with valid nonce+signature → succeeds. Same request replayed (same nonce) → rejected. Request without nonce → rejected. 2. **Anti-replay on high-risk admin mutations:** Integration test — `POST /admin/users` with signed JSON request → succeeds. Same request without signing headers → rejected. Replay with same nonce → rejected. 3. **Stacking/mutual-exclusion enforcement in approval:** Unit test — set up two campaigns with conflicting stacking rules → approve second campaign → approval rejected with stacking conflict error. Set up campaign with no conflicts → approval succeeds. 4. **Analytics issuance with filters:** Integration test — create campaigns in different stores/dates with coupons → call analytics with specific store+date filter → assert issuance only includes matching coupons. Call without filters → includes all. 5. **Weekly restore drill scheduling:** Unit test — verify `weeklyRestoreDrill` calls `testRestoreLatest` and logs audit entry on success. Verify it logs error on failure. 6. **Encryption coverage:** Unit test — for each newly encrypted field: save entity → read raw DB value (via native query) → confirm it's not plaintext. Decrypt via service → confirms original value. --- ## Final Verification (Docker Only) After all changes: ```bash docker compose down docker compose up --build ``` Confirm app starts at http://localhost:8080. Log in with `admin / Admin@Retail2024!` and verify: - Approval queue: approve action on a dual-approval item works (nonce/signature enforced) - Admin user form: submission works with signing (converted to JSON fetch) - Try approving a campaign with conflicting stacking rules → see validation error - Analytics dashboard shows filtered issuance metrics - Restricted action buttons show disabled+tooltip instead of being hidden - All previously fixed flows still work Then: ```bash docker compose -f docker-compose.test.yml run --build test ``` All existing and new tests must pass. --- ## Login Credentials (for manual verification) | Role | Username | Password | |---|---|---| | Administrator | admin | Admin@Retail2024! | | Operations | ops | Ops@Retail2024! | | Reviewer | reviewer | Review@Retail2024! | | Finance | finance | Finance@Retail2024! | | Customer Service | cs | CsUser@Retail2024! | --- ## Audit Report (R4) — Attached for Reference Below is the full audit report these fixes address. The auditor identified 5 High, 2 Medium, and 1 Low issue. All are addressed above. ### High Issues 1. Anti-replay/signing does not protect the actual approval completion endpoint used by UI (`NonceValidationFilter.java:93`, `ApprovalController.java:77`, `queue.html:86`) 2. Request-signing/nonce broadly bypassed for admin browser form mutations (`NonceValidationFilter.java:105,110`, `RequestSigningFilter.java:95,100`) 3. Approval workflow does not enforce stacking/mutual-exclusion verification (`CouponService.java:110` — defined but never called in approval flow) 4. Analytics issuance metric semantically incorrect and ignores filters (`AnalyticsService.java:37` — `findAll()` sum ignoring store/date) 5. Weekly restore-test automation not implemented (`RestoreService.java:100` — method exists but unscheduled) ### Medium Issues 6. "Why blocked" permission explanation inconsistent across screens (some disabled+tooltip, many hidden via `sec:authorize`) 7. Encryption-at-rest narrowly applied to backup file path only (`BackupRecord.java:31`) ### Low Issues 8. README Docker-centric with no local fallback (`README.md:5,12`)

1	# Security Design Decisions
2	
3	This document records security-relevant design choices that are not obvious from the
4	code alone. Each entry explains the rule, the tradeoff, and the compensating controls.
5	
6	## 1. Admin browser-form POSTs are exempt from nonce + signing
7	
8	**Rule.** `NonceValidationFilter` and `RequestSigningFilter` bypass requests to
9	`/admin/**` that carry `Content-Type: application/x-www-form-urlencoded`. All other
10	content types under `/admin/**` (JSON, multipart/form-data, plain text) are subject to
11	the full anti-replay + HMAC signature check.
12	
13	**Why not full signing for browser forms?** Admin UI actions are submitted by the
14	Thymeleaf forms in `templates/admin/*.html`. Generating per-request `X-Nonce`,
15	`X-Timestamp`, `X-Signature` headers from the browser would require every admin form
16	to be HTMX-driven with client-side signing JS and a nonce-issuance endpoint — a large
17	piece of work that does not materially raise the bar for our threat model. The browser
18	surface is already protected by a stack of independent controls (below). We revisit
19	this decision if any of those controls weakens.
20	
21	**Compensating controls** (all active and testable):
22	
23	1. **CSRF** — Spring Security's `CookieCsrfTokenRepository.withHttpOnlyFalse()` protects
24	   every POST. A missing/incorrect CSRF token yields HTTP 403 at the filter chain level
25	   before any controller runs. Covered by `SecurityIntegrationTest.postWithoutCsrfRejected`.
26	2. **Session auth** — Admin endpoints require an authenticated session. Covered by
27	   `SecurityIntegrationTest.anonymousAccessRedirectsToLogin`.
28	3. **ROLE_ADMIN** — Every controller method under `AdminController` is gated by class-
29	   level `@PreAuthorize("hasRole('ADMIN')")`, and `SecurityConfig` additionally enforces
30	   URL-level `.requestMatchers("/admin/**").hasRole("ADMIN")`. Double enforcement.
31	   Covered by `SecurityIntegrationTest.opsUserCannotAccessAdmin` and related tests.
32	4. **Audit log on every mutation** — `AdminController` calls `AuditLogService.log(...)`
33	   (directly or via downstream services) for every user/backup/role/alert mutation.
34	   Covered by `AuditLogIntegrationTest`.
35	5. **Rate limit** — `RateLimitFilter` throttles authenticated users to 60 req/min
36	   (10/min for export), per `application.yml` `app.rate-limit.*`. A brute-forcer who
37	   steals a session still hits the rate limit.
38	6. **Pre-auth lockout + CAPTCHA** — `PreAuthLockoutFilter` prevents credential
39	   stuffing from ever reaching the admin surface.
40	7. **Tight bypass shape** — Only `application/x-www-form-urlencoded` is exempt.
41	   `multipart/form-data` is NOT exempt, because there are no admin file-upload forms
42	   today, so any multipart POST to `/admin/**` must be a programmatic caller and is
43	   required to carry valid nonce + signature headers. Covered by
44	   `AdminFilterBypassTest.{signingFilterRejectsAdminMultipartWithoutSignature,nonceFilterRejectsAdminMultipartWithoutHeaders}`.
45	
46	**What is still protected by nonce + signing?**
47	
48	- JSON POSTs under `/admin/**` (API clients).
49	- Multipart POSTs under `/admin/**`.
50	- `POST /approval/dual-approve/**` (nonce-protected).
51	- Any future `/api/**` prefix.
52	
53	**When to revisit.** Implement full browser-side signing if:
54	
55	- CSRF protection is downgraded or moved to opt-in.
56	- The session model changes (stateless JWTs, etc.) such that session auth no longer
57	  provides per-request binding.
58	- Admin endpoints start accepting cross-origin POSTs.
59	- The audit log gains a mutation surface (today it is immutable — see #2 below).
60	
61	## 2. audit_logs is immutable by construction AND enforced at the DB
62	
63	- The `AuditLog` entity has no `@Setter`. Instances are constructed via `@Builder`.
64	- `AuditLogRepository` extends `Repository<AuditLog, Long>` directly instead of
65	  `JpaRepository`, so `delete*` and `saveAll` methods are not exposed.
66	- Flyway migration `V14__audit_log_immutability.sql` installs a MySQL trigger that
67	  rejects UPDATE and DELETE on `audit_logs` with a SQLSTATE 45000 error. Even a direct
68	  SQL client with full DB credentials cannot tamper with an existing row.
69	- The only legitimate write path is `AuditLogService.log(...)`, which always
70	  constructs a new row.
71	
72	## 3. At-rest encryption uses AES-256-GCM with an env-injected master key
73	
74	- `EncryptionService` derives a 256-bit AES key from `app.encryption.key` via SHA-256.
75	- `EncryptedStringConverter` is a JPA `AttributeConverter<String, String>` applied with
76	  `@Convert(converter = EncryptedStringConverter.class)` on sensitive String fields.
77	  First target: `BackupRecord.filePath`.
78	- The master key has NO plaintext fallback in `application.yml` — it must come from
79	  `ENCRYPTION_KEY` (or the legacy `APP_ENCRYPTION_KEY`) env var. `docker-compose.yml`
80	  supplies a local-dev default so `docker compose up --build` still works without
81	  manual configuration. In production, inject from a secret manager.
82	- Because the IV is random per call, encryption is non-deterministic. Encrypted
83	  columns cannot be used in `WHERE` clauses — pick fields for encryption that are
84	  only looked up by primary key.
85

# audit_report-2 Fix Check (Static Re-Inspection)

## Re-check Verdict

- **Overall:** Most previously reported issues are now statically addressed.
- **Result summary:**
  - **Fixed:** 6
  - **Partially Fixed:** 1
  - **Not Fixed / Still Partial:** 1

Static-only boundary: this check is based on source/templates/docs inspection only; no runtime execution, Docker, or tests were run.

---

## Issue-by-Issue Status

### 1) High — Anti-replay/signing do not protect actual high-risk approval completion endpoint

- **Previous:** Fail
- **Current status:** **Fixed (statically)**
- **Evidence:**
  - Nonce filter now explicitly covers `/approval/{id}/approve-first|approve-second`: `src/main/java/com/meridian/retail/security/NonceValidationFilter.java:95-103`
  - UI still posts to `/{id}/approve-second`, but those forms are now nonce-armed (`class="js-nonce-form"`): `src/main/resources/templates/approval/queue.html:75-99`
  - Nonce injection script exists and auto-arms privileged approval/admin forms: `src/main/resources/static/js/nonce-form.js:1-121`
  - Script is globally loaded in base layout: `src/main/resources/templates/layout/base.html:72-73`
  - Nonce endpoints exist (`/approval/nonce`, `/admin/nonce`): `src/main/java/com/meridian/retail/controller/NonceController.java:29-44`

### 2) High — Request-signing/nonce controls broadly bypassed for admin browser form mutations

- **Previous:** Partial Fail
- **Current status:** **Partially Fixed**
- **What is fixed:**
  - Nonce blanket bypass removed (`shouldNotFilter` returns false), so admin form POSTs now pass nonce validation path: `src/main/java/com/meridian/retail/security/NonceValidationFilter.java:121-128`
- **What remains:**
  - Request-signing filter still bypasses `application/x-www-form-urlencoded` under `/admin/**`: `src/main/java/com/meridian/retail/security/RequestSigningFilter.java:95-104`
- **Conclusion:** nonce side of finding fixed; signing side still intentionally bypassed for browser forms.

### 3) High — Approval workflow does not enforce stacking/mutual-exclusion verification

- **Previous:** Fail
- **Current status:** **Fixed (statically)**
- **Evidence:**
  - Approval paths now call pre-approval conflict validation: `src/main/java/com/meridian/retail/service/ApprovalService.java:106-111`, `149-151`, `196-203`
  - New validation implementation for approval-time coupon conflicts: `src/main/java/com/meridian/retail/service/CouponService.java:170-228`

### 4) High — Analytics issuance metric semantically incorrect and not filter-aware

- **Previous:** Fail
- **Current status:** **Fixed (statically)**
- **Evidence:**
  - Issuance now uses filter-aware repository aggregation (store/date overlap): `src/main/java/com/meridian/retail/service/AnalyticsService.java:30-40`
  - New repository query for filtered issuance proxy: `src/main/java/com/meridian/retail/repository/CouponRepository.java:24-33`

### 5) High — Weekly restore-test automation not implemented

- **Previous:** Fail
- **Current status:** **Fixed (statically)**
- **Evidence:**
  - Weekly scheduled restore drill added: `src/main/java/com/meridian/retail/backup/RestoreService.java:149-167` (`@Scheduled(cron = "0 0 3 ? * SUN")`)

### 6) Medium — “Why blocked” permission explanation inconsistent across screens

- **Previous:** Partial Fail
- **Current status:** **Not Fixed / Still Partial**
- **Evidence:**
  - Some pages still provide explicit disabled+reason affordance: `src/main/resources/templates/campaign/list.html:16-22`, `111-116`
  - Sidebar still hides restricted nav items with `sec:authorize` and no inline “why blocked” context: `src/main/resources/templates/layout/sidebar.html:1-66`
- **Conclusion:** inconsistency remains.

### 7) Medium — Encryption-at-rest narrowly applied only to backup file path

- **Previous:** Partial Fail
- **Current status:** **Fixed (for the previously reported narrow-scope defect)**
- **Evidence:**
  - Encryption converter now also applied to `BackupRecord.notes`: `src/main/java/com/meridian/retail/entity/BackupRecord.java:56`
  - Additional encrypted field in anomaly module: `src/main/java/com/meridian/retail/entity/AnomalyAlert.java:31`
- **Note:** Whether encryption coverage is fully sufficient to all threat models remains a separate architecture question, but the specific “only backup file path” finding is no longer accurate.

### 8) Low — README Docker-centric, no optional local fallback

- **Previous:** Partial
- **Current status:** **Fixed**
- **Evidence:**
  - Optional non-Docker local section now documented: `README.md:29-52`

---

## Final Fix-Check Conclusion

- The project has materially improved against the prior `audit_report-2` findings.
- **Remaining material gap from this list:** Issue #2 (partially fixed) and Issue #6 (still partial).
- Manual/runtime confirmation is still required for behavioral effectiveness of the nonce JS flow and operational restore drill execution cadence.

{"replace_all": false, "file_path": "/home/abdelah/Documents/eaglepoint/TASK-31/repo/SecurityDesignDecisions.md", "old_string": "## 1. Privileged browser POSTs use nonce form fields, not signing headers

**Rule (R4 update).** `NonceValidationFilter` no longer carries any blanket form-encoded
bypass. Every privileged POST under `/admin/**`, `/approval/dual-approve/**`, and the
two dual-approval completion endpoints `/approval/{id}/approve-first` and
`/approval/{id}/approve-second` must carry a valid nonce + timestamp pair, accepted
from EITHER the `X-Nonce`/`X-Timestamp` headers (programmatic clients) OR the `_nonce`
and `_timestamp` form parameters (browser forms enriched by `static/js/nonce-form.js`
before submission). The two encodings are equivalent from an anti-replay standpoint —
both consult the same `used_nonces` table.

`RequestSigningFilter` still bypasses `application/x-www-form-urlencoded` POSTs under
`/admin/**` because HMAC-SHA256 signing requires a shared secret that cannot live in a
browser without leaking the key in JavaScript. JSON / multipart callers under
`/admin/**` are still subject to the full HMAC signature check.", "new_string": "## 1. Privileged browser POSTs use nonce + server-issued signature form fields

**Rule (R4 audit re-check update).** Both `NonceValidationFilter` and
`RequestSigningFilter` are now active for every privileged browser POST — there is no
form-encoded bypass on either. Browser submissions reach the controller layer only when
they carry all three of:

- `_nonce` (single-use UUID, persisted to `used_nonces` after first use)
- `_timestamp` (epoch milliseconds, must be within ±5 minutes of server time)
- `_signature` (hex HMAC-SHA256 over `method + "\n" + path + "\n" + timestamp + "\n" + nonce`)

The signature is **issued by the server**, never computed in the browser, by
`POST /admin/sign-form` (and `POST /approval/sign-form`). These endpoints are gated by
Spring Security (`ROLE_ADMIN` / `ROLE_REVIEWER` + authenticated session + CSRF) and
return only the HMAC over the requested canonical tuple. The HMAC secret therefore
never leaves the JVM. `static/js/nonce-form.js` orchestrates the three-step flow
(fetch nonce → fetch signature → submit form).

**Why omit the body hash from the form-mode canonical?** The body itself contains the
`_signature` parameter — including the hash would create a chicken-and-egg loop. Body
integrity in form mode is provided by:

1. CSRF token (CookieCsrfTokenRepository) bound to the session.
2. Single-use nonce that prevents replay with a tampered body.
3. Same-origin policy + session auth that bind the request to the user.
4. Audit log on every mutation (immutable, DB-trigger-enforced).

Programmatic JSON / multipart callers continue to use the **header mode** of
`RequestSigningFilter`, which DOES include the body hash:

```
HMAC = HMAC-SHA256( method + "\n" + path + "\n" + X-Timestamp + "\n" + SHA256(body, hex) )
sent as X-Signature header alongside X-Nonce / X-Timestamp.
```

The two modes are mutually exclusive on a per-request basis: if `X-Signature` is
present the filter uses header mode, otherwise it falls back to form mode and reads
`_signature` / `_nonce` / `_timestamp` from the parameters."}

requires the shared secret to be embedded in JavaScript, which trivially leaks it to
any user with devtools. Nonces are different: they are server-issued, single-use, and
stored in a database table — there is no client secret to leak. Pairing nonce + CSRF
+ session auth + audit log gives us replay protection in addition to all the existing
compensating controls below.", "new_string": "**Why server-side signing instead of browser-side HMAC?** Browser-side HMAC requires
the shared secret to live in JavaScript, which trivially leaks it to any user with
devtools. By moving the signing operation to a server endpoint gated by Spring
Security, the HMAC secret never leaves the JVM. The endpoint is effectively a
"sign this canonical tuple if you are an authenticated admin" service — equivalent to
how short-lived OAuth tokens work, scoped to a single nonce + timestamp."}

echo

Both R4 audit gaps closed. Summary:

**Issue #2 — RequestSigningFilter form-encoded bypass (now fully fixed)**
- Dropped the `application/x-www-form-urlencoded` shouldNotFilter bypass.
- Filter now supports two canonical modes:
  - **Header mode** (programmatic): existing `X-Signature` + body-hash canonical.
  - **Form mode** (browser): `_signature` form param over `method+path+timestamp+nonce` (body hash omitted to avoid chicken-and-egg with `_signature` itself; replay protection comes from the single-use nonce already consumed by `NonceValidationFilter`).
- New `POST /admin/sign-form` and `POST /approval/sign-form` endpoints (in `NonceController`) issue signatures server-side, gated by Spring Security so the HMAC secret never lives in the browser.
- Both filters' `appliesTo` now exclude the sign-form endpoints to prevent a signing loop.
- `nonce-form.js` updated: for `/admin/**` forms it now performs three steps — fetch nonce → fetch signature → submit with `_nonce`/`_timestamp`/`_signature` hidden fields.
- New tests in `AdminFilterBypassTest`: rejects form POST without signature, accepts with valid signature, rejects bad signature, skips sign-form endpoint. New `SecurityIntegrationTest.adminFormPostWithSignedNonceFieldsPassesAllFilters` and `adminFormPostWithoutSignatureRejectedBySigningFilter` verify the integration stack.
- `SecurityDesignDecisions.md` rewritten to describe the new contract.

**Issue #6 — Sidebar permission UX (now fully fixed)**
- Every restricted nav item in `templates/layout/sidebar.html` is rendered for all users: authorized users see the active link, unauthorized users see a `<span class="nav-disabled">` with a `title=` tooltip explaining the required role.
- New `.nav-disabled` style added to `static/css/custom.css` (greyed text, lock icon, `cursor: not-allowed`), matching the existing in-page disabled+tooltip pattern.

**Verification**
- `docker compose -f docker-compose.test.yml run --build test` → all unit tests + 25 integration tests pass.
- `docker compose up --build` boots cleanly to Flyway v15, `/health` returns `{"status":"UP"}`.

**File:** `repo/src/main/resources/application-test.yml`

```diff
 app:

+  # ──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────

+  # so these values are never loaded outside tests and the real ENCRYPTION_KEY /
+  # HMAC_KEY env vars from docker-compose.yml continue to drive prod behaviour.

+  # in EncryptionService — they are NEVER reused in any other environment.

+  # provide an exact 32-character string so the bytes match what AES-256 expects
+  # if any future code path skips the SHA-256 stretch.
+  encryption:
+    key: "ci-test-key-DO-NOT-USE-IN-PROD!!"   # 32 chars, test profile only
+  signing:
+    secret: "ci-test-hmac-DO-NOT-USE-IN-PROD!"  # 32 chars, test profile only
```

**Why this is the cleanest fix**
- `application-test.yml` already exists in `src/main/resources/` and is loaded automatically by Spring Boot whenever `spring.profiles.active=test`. No `@TestPropertySource` sprinkled across test classes; one place, one rule.
- `docker-compose.yml` sets `SPRING_PROFILES_ACTIVE=docker`, so the `test` profile (and these dummy keys) is **never** activated in production. The real `ENCRYPTION_KEY`/`HMAC_KEY` env vars in `application.yml`'s `${ENCRYPTION_KEY:${APP_ENCRYPTION_KEY:}}` chain still take precedence in Docker/prod.
- The production key path stays exactly as-is (no hard-coding of the prod key anywhere).

**Test keys (32 chars each, AES-256 compatible)**
| Property | Value | Length |
|---|---|---|
| `app.encryption.key` | `ci-test-key-DO-NOT-USE-IN-PROD!!` | 32 |
| `app.signing.secret` | `ci-test-hmac-DO-NOT-USE-IN-PROD!` | 32 |

The `DO-NOT-USE-IN-PROD` substring is intentional — anyone grepping for these strings in production logs will spot the test key immediately. `EncryptionService` runs them through SHA-256 to derive the 256-bit AES key regardless, so length is not strictly required by the algorithm, but I kept exactly 32 chars to match what AES-256 expects raw, so any future refactor that drops the SHA-256 stretch still works.
